content:
# Factors affecting mercury capillary pressure measurements

**Summary**: The thesis investigates factors affecting mercury capillary pressure measurements in Berea sandstone and carbonate rocks, specifically focusing on sample size, epoxy coating, and equilibrium time. It evaluates these factors' impact on permeability estimation using Katz and Thompson's method versus Swanson's correlation, and utilizes the J-function to correlate capillary pressure-saturation data.

---

## Research Problem

The study addresses the variability in mercury capillary pressure curves caused by experimental conditions (sample size, coating, time) and how these variations affect the accuracy of derived reservoir properties like permeability and pore throat distribution in both sandstone and carbonate systems.

## Objectives

- Evaluate the influence of sample size on the capillary pressure saturation relation.
- To determine the influence of equilibrium time (slow vs. rapid injection/ejection) on the resulting capillary pressure data.
- Evaluate the effect of epoxy coating.
- Determine parameters influencing the forms of capillary pressure curves beyond those already studied.
- Use capillary pressure data to calculate permeability and compare with previous studies.
- Correlate experimental data using J-function or an extended J-function.

## Methods

Mercury injection experiments were performed using a volumetric pump, high-pressure pycnometer, and pressure control panel. Core samples (Berea, Bentheimer, and carbonate) were prepared in various sizes (2cm, 4cm, 6cm) and with/without epoxy coating. Helium porosity and gas permeability were measured prior to mercury tests. Two injection rates (slow and rapid) were used to test equilibrium effects. The Katz and Thompson method was applied to calculate permeability from the capillary pressure curves using critical length analysis.

## Data and Materials

32 core samples in total, including Berea sandstone (10 samples), Bentheimer sandstone (10 samples), and carbonate rocks (12 samples). Samples were tested with varying lengths (2cm, 4cm, 6cm) and coating conditions. Data included mercury injection/ejection curves, pore throat size distributions, and permeability measurements.

## Key Findings

- Injection capillary pressure curves for Berea sandstone are essentially independent of sample size regardless of coating or equilibrium time. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 77]])
- For coated carbonate rock samples, injection capillary pressure curves depend on sample size; as size increased, curves moved to lower saturation and displacement pressure decreased. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 22]])
- Katz and Thompson's percolation theory gave a standard deviation error of 45% for permeability estimation, while Swanson's correlation approach yielded an error of 72%. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 22]])
- Pore throat sorting (PTS) was used as a parameter in the extended J-function for sandstone rocks to improve correlation. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 22]])
- Critical length (L) was considered in the correlation function for carbonate samples with permeability ranging from 20 md to 800 md. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 22]])
- Approximately 80% of pore throats in both Berea and Bentheimer samples were in the range of 10 to 60 $\mu$m. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 53]])
- Berea samples had slightly larger pore throat diameters and more microporosity than Bentheimer samples. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 53]])
- Withdrawal capillary pressure curves show that residual mercury saturation increases as the sample size increases, particularly in larger core samples where more time is required to extract mercury. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 83]])
- For carbonate rocks, the injection curve moves to lower pressure ranges as the pore throat diameter increases (larger sample sizes). ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 89]])
- quotation_of_the_moment_is_not_applicable_here_but_finding_is_that_for_carbonate_rocks, the residual saturation decreases as the pore throat of the sample diameter increases. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991]])
- page_number_is_89 ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991]])
- Katz and Thompson's method provided a better correlation for permeability (45% deviation) compared to Swanson's method (72%). ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 182]])
- Sample size variation did not significantly affect injection capillary pressure curves for sandstone, but carbonate samples showed strong dependence on pore throat diameter and permeability. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 220]])
- For rapid withdrawal rates, residual saturation increased with sample size in sandstone, while unsaturated pore volumes remained constant across all sizes. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 220]])
- Surface coating (epoxy) on carbonate samples shifted the curve to lower saturation and reduced displacement pressure, whereas it had no significant effect on high-permeability sandstone. ([[1991-ali|Ali, 1991, p. 221]])

## Limitations

The number of carbonate samples (8) may not be sufficient to fully evaluate rock characteristics. Results for samples with permeability under 10 md are less accurate due to difficulties in determining inflection points.

## Recommendations and Future Work

Not stated in source
